evidence: CRUD subclasses inconsistently use typed filter models (FilterAssistant, FilterVectorStore, FilterFileObject) while others pass raw dicts directly to base methods (CRUDThread, CRUDRun, CRUDMessage, CRUDAPIKey)

Router parameter ordering is inconsistent: session appears first in create_assistant(session, request), last in create_thread(request, session), middle in create_run(thread_id, session, request), and first again in delete_vector_store_file(session, vector_store_id, file_id)
Composer.generate_response returns Run | StreamingResponse depending on request.stream flag; create_run in runs.py returns Run | StreamingResponse
Most route handlers raise HTTPException but do not document error contracts in docstrings; only modify_assistant includes a Raises section
CRUDBase.delete catches bare Exception and returns False, silently swallowing all database errors
backend/helpers.py mixes async generators (recv_completion, recv_chat) with sync utilities (grpc_chat_role, read_chunks, object_or_default) in the same public module

Database backends diverge: TursoQueryBuilder exposes neq/gt/gte/lt/lte/order/limit/range that SupabaseQueryBuilder lacks; TursoClient.create(base_url, auth_token) vs SupabaseClient.create(supabase_url, supabase_key)
cowabunga tokens router bypasses dependency injection: count_tokens_route(text: str) hardcodes model config lookup instead of using Depends(get_model_config) like sibling routers
SDK serve() uses hasattr(o, 'ChatComplete') dynamic dispatch, making the public contract depend on runtime object shape rather than static types
grpc_client.py functions (stream_completion, completion, etc.) call gRPC stubs without documented failure modes or retry contracts
stream_generate_message_for_thread in composer.py takes 8 positional parameters, exceeding the heuristic API-boundary threshold

api_surface_coherence.dimension_character: The API surface has recognizable structural patterns—async FastAPI routers, CRUD inheritance, and dependency injection—that provide a coherent foundation. However, parameter ordering drifts across sibling routes, CRUD subclasses diverge in filter typing, database backends expose unequal query surfaces, and several modules mix sync/async utilities or rely on dynamic runtime dispatch. Error contracts are largely undocumented, and silent exception swallowing in CRUDBase.delete erodes developer trust at the boundary.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase scores slightly above average because high-level patterns (CRUD base, async routers, FastAPI DI) are consistent enough to be learnable. It falls short of good because parameter ordering drifts across sibling modules, database client abstractions leak backend-specific capabilities, the SDK uses dynamic hasattr dispatch rather than explicit protocols, and several public modules mix sync and async utilities without clear boundaries. Missing error contracts and CRUDBase's silent exception swallowing further degrade predictability.

issues.summary: CRUD subclasses use inconsistent filter types: some typed models, some raw dicts
evidence: CRUDAssistant, CRUDVectorStore, and CRUDFileObject override get/list/delete to accept typed filter models (FilterAssistant, FilterVectorStore, FilterFileObject)
CRUDThread, CRUDRun, and CRUDMessage inherit base methods directly and pass raw dict filters like {'id': thread_id}
CRUDAPIKey overrides get/list but still accepts dict | None, not a typed filter model
issues.suggestion: Standardize all CRUD subclasses to use typed filter models that convert to dict internally, or remove typed filter subclasses and consistently use dicts across the layer. Pick one pattern and apply it uniformly.

issues.summary: Session parameter position drifts inconsistently across route handlers
evidence: create_assistant(session, request) → session first
create_thread(request, session) → session last
create_run(thread_id, session, request) → session middle
create_message(thread_id, request, session) → session last
delete_vector_store_file(session, vector_store_id, file_id) → session first (unlike siblings in same file)
issues.suggestion: Adopt a consistent parameter ordering convention for all route handlers, such as path parameters first, then session, then request body. Apply consistently across all router modules.

issues.summary: Public functions return union types based on runtime flags rather than separate endpoints
evidence: Composer.generate_response returns Run | StreamingResponse based on request.stream flag
create_run in runs.py returns Run | StreamingResponse based on runtime branching
chat_complete returns either a ChatCompletionResponse or a StreamingResponse based on req.stream
complete in completions.py similarly branches on req.stream with no return type annotation
issues.suggestion: Either split streaming and non-streaming into separate functions with distinct return types, or document the union return type explicitly and ensure callers handle both cases. Adding return type annotations to chat_complete and complete would also help.

issues.summary: Most route handlers do not document error conditions in docstrings despite raising HTTPExceptions
evidence: create_assistant raises HTTPException with 400 and 500 status codes but docstring only says 'Create an assistant'
create_thread raises HTTPException with 400 and 500 but docstring lacks Raises section
Only modify_assistant in assistants.py includes a Raises section documenting HTTPException conditions
grpc_client.py functions call gRPC stubs without documented failure modes or retry contracts
Broad try/except Exception blocks wrap helper calls and re-raise as 500, but these error contracts are invisible to API consumers reading the docstrings
issues.suggestion: Add Raises sections to all public route handler docstrings documenting the HTTPException conditions (status codes and triggers). Document gRPC client failure modes (connection errors, timeout behavior) in grpc_client.py.

issues.summary: CRUDBase.delete catches all exceptions and silently returns False
evidence: CRUDBase.delete contains 'except Exception: return False' which hides database connection errors, permission errors, and syntax errors from callers
issues.suggestion: Let specific exceptions propagate or wrap them in a custom CRUD exception that preserves the original cause. Only return False when the delete genuinely matched zero rows.

issues.summary: Public helper module mixes async and sync functions without clear boundary
evidence: async def recv_completion(stream, model) and async def recv_chat(stream, model) are async SSE generators
def grpc_chat_role(role: str) -> lfai.ChatRole, def read_chunks(file: BinaryIO, chunk_size: int), and def object_or_default(obj, _default) are synchronous utilities
All six functions are exported from the same module surface with no subpackage or naming convention separating async from sync utilities
issues.suggestion: Split backend/helpers.py into two modules (e.g., backend/async_helpers.py and backend/sync_helpers.py) or adopt a naming convention that makes async/sync boundary explicit at the import site.

issues.summary: Database client backends expose unequal query surfaces and different factory parameter names
evidence: TursoQueryBuilder adds neq, gt, gte, lt, lte, order, limit, range beyond the QueryBuilder ABC
SupabaseQueryBuilder only wraps the Supabase query object and does not expose the extra filter methods
TursoClient.create(base_url, auth_token) vs SupabaseClient.create(supabase_url, supabase_key) — parameter names differ even though semantic roles are identical
Callers depending on backend-specific query capabilities will fail silently when the factory returns the other backend
issues.suggestion: Expand the QueryBuilder ABC to include the common filter methods, implement them for both backends, and align factory parameter names (e.g., url and key for both). Alternatively, document which query operations are backend-specific.

issues.summary: Cowabunga tokens router bypasses FastAPI dependency injection used by sibling routers
evidence: count_tokens_route(text: str) hardcodes model config lookup internally instead of accepting model_config: ModelConfig = Depends(get_model_config)
count.py tokens(session, model_config, request) properly injects both session and model_config via Depends
The bypass makes the tokens endpoint less testable and inconsistent with the dependency-injection pattern used across all other route modules
issues.suggestion: Refactor count_tokens_route to accept model_config via Depends(get_model_config), matching the pattern in count.py and the OpenAI routers.

issues.summary: SDK serve() and LLM decorator construct interfaces dynamically at runtime
evidence: serve(o, host='0.0.0.0', port=50051) uses hasattr(o, 'ChatComplete') to decide which gRPC services to register
LLM decorator dynamically creates NewClass with ChatComplete, ChatCompleteStream, Complete, CompleteStream, CountTokens methods at runtime
The effective public API of decorated classes is not statically discoverable; type checkers and IDEs cannot infer the generated interface
issues.suggestion: Define an explicit Protocol or ABC that documents the required methods, and use isinstance checks against the protocol instead of hasattr. This makes the contract statically verifiable and improves IDE support for SDK consumers.

root_causes: Multiple contributors working on different router/CRUD modules without a documented API surface convention for parameter ordering and filter typing
Streaming vs non-streaming modeled as a single endpoint to match OpenAI API spec, causing overloaded return types
Database abstraction added incrementally (Turso after Supabase) without fully equalizing the backend-specific query surfaces
SDK evolved from internal prototype to public package without converting dynamic runtime patterns to static protocols

add.description: All FastAPI route handlers across openai/ and cowabunga/ routers are consistently async, with no sync/async mixing at the router boundary.

add.header: CRUD inheritance pattern provides structural consistency
add.description: Despite filter-typing drift, the CRUDBase generic class gives the data layer a predictable shape (create/get/list/update/delete) that is followed by all subclasses.
Session and model_config dependencies are injected via Depends across most route modules, showing a consistent integration pattern with the framework.

add.header: OpenAI typedef models follow consistent Pydantic patterns
add.description: All OpenAI-compatible request/response types use Pydantic BaseModel with consistent field naming and validation, providing a coherent serialization surface.

add.header: Database backends expose unequal query surfaces
add.description: TursoQueryBuilder has filter methods (neq, gt, gte, lt, lte, order, limit, range) that SupabaseQueryBuilder lacks, so callers cannot rely on a uniform query API regardless of which backend is configured.

add.header: SDK uses runtime dynamic dispatch instead of static protocols
add.description: serve() and the LLM decorator use hasattr and runtime class construction, which means the public SDK contract is only discoverable at runtime and cannot be type-checked statically.
